title: Reference-Dependent Risk Attitudes
abstract: We use Kőszegi and Rabin's (2006) model of reference-dependent utility, and an extension of it that applies to decisions with delayed consequences, to study preferences over monetary risk. Because our theory equates the reference point with recent probabilistic beliefs about outcomes, it predicts specific ways in which the environment influences attitudes toward modest-scale risk. It replicates “classical” prospect theory—including the prediction of distaste for insuring losses—when exposure to risk is a surprise, but implies first-order risk aversion when a risk, and the possibility of insuring it, are anticipated. A prior expectation to take on risk decreases aversion to both the anticipated and additional risk. For large-scale risk, the model allows for standard “consumption utility” to dominate reference-dependent “gain-loss utility,” generating nearly identical risk aversion across situations. (JEL D81)
summary_en: Building on their 2006 reference-dependent model, the authors develop a theory of decisions with risk. A key twist: attitudes toward risk depend on whether the risk is a surprise or expected. An unexpected risk produces the classical prospect-theory pattern (people refuse actuarially fair insurance in the loss domain). But an expected risk — and the possibility of insuring it — brings first-order risk aversion. Anticipating risk-taking in advance reduces the aversion to both the anticipated and any additional risk. For large-scale risk, consumption utility dominates and risk attitudes look standard.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian insurance-market regulation (MNB) and pension-scheme default design: the paper's prediction that expected vs unexpected framing produces different risk-taking is directly relevant. Mandatory or auto-enrolled pension plans should show different savings and risk-portfolio behaviour than opt-in plans, even at the same participation rate. For individual-insurance markets, how products are framed (as loss protection vs as planned investment) changes take-up. Abstract; specific product-design implications require experimental follow-up.
